_purpose: Seasonal calendar for the AL Tennessee Valley cluster. Month-by-month agricultural, social, economic, and weather patterns across three eras: the antebellum cotton calendar (1809-1860), the postbellum tenant/subsistence calendar (1865-1933), and the TVA/defense transformation era (1933-1960). The Tennessee Valley's seasonal rhythm was cotton on the bottomland, corn and tobacco on Sand Mountain, and — after TVA — a calendar increasingly shaped by lake recreation, federal employment, and the arsenal's year-round industrial schedule.
display_name: AL Tennessee Valley — Seasonal Calendar Profile
primary_economy: Cotton agriculture (bottomland counties 1809-1933); subsistence farming with corn, hogs, and mountain tobacco (hill/mountain counties 1836-1940); TVA construction and federal defense employment (1933-1960)

calendar_eras.description: The bottomland cotton calendar governed life in Madison, Limestone, Morgan, and Lawrence counties. The Tennessee Valley's growing season was slightly shorter than the Deep South — a higher latitude and the elevation of the valley floor meant later springs and earlier frosts. Cotton was king on the bottomland, but corn was the subsistence crop everywhere. The mountain counties (Marshall, DeKalb, Cherokee) followed a different rhythm — corn and hog farming, with small patches of tobacco for cash. Court day at the county seat was the primary civic gathering in both zones. The quarterly circuit court drew people from across the county for legal business, trade, socializing, and gossip. Camp meetings in late summer were the spiritual and social peak of the year.

january.agricultural: Fields dormant. Hog killing in bottomland and mountain farms. Fence repair. Wood cutting. On plantations, enslaved people cleared new ground, split rails, and repaired buildings. Mountain farmers hauled wood and tended livestock.
january.social_civic: Quarterly circuit court sessions in county seats — the primary civic gathering. Court day drew people for legal business, trading, and socializing. Church services on Sunday. Visiting limited by cold and mud.
january.economic: Cotton from the fall harvest being shipped downriver or held for spring prices. Crop-lien credit negotiations with merchants for the new planting season. On Sand Mountain, no cash economy to speak of — January was a subsistence month.
january.weather_conditions: Cold — average 36-42F in the valley, 30-38F on Sand Mountain. Hard freezes common. The Tennessee River could flood in January thaws. Valley roads passable on limestone base; mountain roads ice-locked.
january.narrative_hook: In January {year}, {ancestor_name}'s world in {county} County contracted to the hearth and the barn — the cotton shipped or stored, the hog meat curing in the smokehouse, and the merchant's ledger already open for the new year's credit.

february.agricultural: Late winter fieldwork on warm days. Breaking new ground. Preparing plow gear. On plantations, enslaved people continued clearing land and repairing infrastructure. Mountain farmers tapped maple trees where available and began preparing garden plots.
february.social_civic: Limited social activity. Some church services. Quilting bees and corn shuckings provided winter socializing in mountain communities.
february.economic: Credit deepening at the crossroads store. Seed and supply purchases planned. The economic vulnerability of both planter and yeoman was greatest in late winter — no income since fall, expenses accumulating.
february.weather_conditions: Late winter — 38-48F in the valley, 32-42F on Sand Mountain. Ice storms a major hazard. The Tennessee River rose with winter rains.
february.narrative_hook: February in the Tennessee Valley was when {ancestor_name} counted what was left from the fall cotton money and calculated whether the family could reach planting season — or whether the merchant would have to carry them another month.

march.agricultural: Plowing began in earnest on the bottomland. Cotton beds prepared. Corn planted on warmer days. Garden plots turned. Mountain farms began later — the plateau was still cold. Tobacco plant beds started in mountain counties.
march.social_civic: Spring court sessions. Church services resumed regular attendance. Community gathered for planting. Easter observances.
march.economic: Seed and supply purchases. Crop-lien merchants did peak credit business. Every farm family needed supplies on credit against the fall crop.
march.weather_conditions: Warming — 50-60F in the valley. Dogwood and redbud blooming. Spring rains began — heavy enough to delay plowing on bottomland clay.
march.narrative_hook: March in {county} County was when the plow hit the ground and the year's gamble began — {ancestor_name} breaking bottomland that grew cotton as rich as any in Alabama, or turning thin mountain soil that would grow corn if the rain came right.

april.agricultural: Cotton planting on the bottomland. Corn already up in the valley. Mountain farms planting corn and garden crops. Tobacco transplanted from plant beds to fields in mountain counties.
april.social_civic: Easter services. Circuit rider visits. Planting was communal in mountain communities where neighbors helped neighbors.
april.economic: Full credit extension. The farmer was committed — seed in the ground, credit drawn, no turning back until fall.
april.weather_conditions: Warm — 60-70F in the valley. Late frost could kill cotton seedlings. Mountain temperatures lagged the valley by 5-8 degrees.
april.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} dropped cotton seed in the Tennessee River bottomland in April {year}, {pronoun_subject} was betting on the same equation every valley farmer faced — weather, worm, and the price at the gin.

may.agricultural: Cotton chopping — hoeing between rows to control weeds. First cultivation. Corn growing. On plantations, the enslaved labor force worked from dawn to dusk in the fields. Mountain farms cultivated corn and tended tobacco.
may.social_civic: Road maintenance (corvee labor required by county). Church services. Community work days.
may.economic: No income. Credit accumulating. The lean months began.
may.weather_conditions: Hot — 70-80F in the valley. Increasing humidity. Thunderstorms. Sand Mountain temperatures slightly lower but humidity high.
may.narrative_hook: May was chopping season — {ancestor_name} in the cotton rows from sunrise to dark, hoeing weeds in bottomland soil that grew them as fast as cotton.

june.agricultural: Cotton growing. Second cultivation. Corn tasseling. Wheat harvest in some areas. Mountain tobacco growing.
june.social_civic: Church services. Days lengthening. Social activity increasing as the crop grew on its own.
june.economic: No income. Credit deepening. The summer lean season at its worst for families dependent on the crop-lien system.
june.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 78-86F in the valley. Thunderstorms frequent and violent. The Tennessee River ran low in dry summers; flooded in wet ones.
june.narrative_hook: June in the Tennessee Valley was when the cotton was growing but the merchant's interest was growing faster — {ancestor_name} watched both and could do nothing about either.

july.agricultural: Lay-by time on the bottomland — the cotton growing on its own, little to do until harvest. Mountain farms continued cultivating corn and tobacco. Lay-by was the one break in the agricultural calendar.
july.social_civic: Camp meeting season began in late July. Camp meetings at established grounds drew families from across the county for extended religious services — a week or more of preaching, singing, socializing, and courtship. The Big Spring Camp Ground in Madison County and similar grounds across the region were annual gathering points.
july.economic: No income. Lay-by was the one time a farmer might do odd jobs for cash — hauling, carpentry, blacksmithing.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 80-90F in the valley, 75-85F on Sand Mountain. Drought risk. The humidity on the bottomland was oppressive.
july.narrative_hook: Lay-by in July was the one breath the cotton calendar allowed — {ancestor_name} could stop hoeing and attend the camp meeting, where a week of preaching and singing was the closest thing to vacation the crop would permit.

august.agricultural: Cotton bolls forming and early varieties opening. Harvest preparation. Corn maturing. Mountain tobacco being cut and hung to cure.
august.social_civic: Camp meeting and revival season at peak. Baptist and Methodist revivals drew families for week-long evening services. Courtship season for young people at the revival. The emotional intensity of the revival was the spiritual peak of the year.
august.economic: Anticipation of harvest income. Merchants calculating expected returns against credit extended.
august.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 80-90F. Drought or excessive rain could ruin the crop at this critical stage. Late-season hurricanes from the Gulf could push rain northward into the valley.
august.narrative_hook: August revival at {church_name} in {county} County was when {ancestor_name} heard something other than the crop's demands — the preacher's voice carrying through the summer dark, promising something the cotton could never deliver.

september.agricultural: Cotton picking began on the bottomland. The entire labor force — free and enslaved — worked the fields from dawn to dusk. Mountain corn harvest. Tobacco curing continued.
september.social_civic: Picking consumed all social time. Church attendance dropped as harvest demanded every available hand.
september.economic: First income of the year as cotton reached the gin. The crop-lien debt was settled against the crop's proceeds. On Sand Mountain, corn harvest determined whether the family would eat through winter.
september.weather_conditions: Moderating — 72-82F. First cool mornings arrived. Hurricane remnants could bring rain that damaged open cotton bolls.
september.narrative_hook: September was the reckoning — {ancestor_name} in the cotton field from dawn to dark, every boll picked by hand, the weight of each sack measured against the debt at the crossroads store.

october.agricultural: Cotton picking at peak on the bottomland. Corn harvested and cribbed. Hog fattening on mast in both valley and mountain. Mountain tobacco being stripped and bundled for market.
october.social_civic: Fall circuit court session. County fairs where they existed. Hunting season — deer, turkey, bear in the mountains.
october.economic: Primary income month. Cotton sold at the gin or shipped downriver. The year's economic fate determined. Mountain families marketed small quantities of tobacco and livestock.
october.weather_conditions: Cool and pleasant — 58-68F. First frost by late October. The Tennessee Valley's most comfortable month.
october.narrative_hook: October was when {ancestor_name} stood at the gin in {county} County and learned whether the year's work had cleared the debt or left the family deeper in — the cotton weighed, the merchant's book consulted, and the verdict delivered in pounds and cents.

november.agricultural: Late cotton picking on the bottomland. Fields cleaned. Winter preparation — firewood, fence repair, barn storage. Mountain families completed final harvest work.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving observances. Hunting season at peak — deer, turkey, small game provided winter protein.
november.economic: Final cotton sales. Debt settlement with the merchant. Planning for next year's credit line.
november.weather_conditions: Cold arriving — 44-54F in the valley, 38-48F on Sand Mountain. First hard frost by mid-November.
november.narrative_hook: By November {year}, {ancestor_name} knew the year's verdict — the cotton money counted against the merchant's book, and the arithmetic determined whether Christmas brought breathing room or another year of starting behind.

december.agricultural: Hog killing — the central food event of the year in both valley and mountain. Meat curing and processing. Fields dormant. Firewood cutting.
december.social_civic: Christmas — the paramount social event. Church services. Visiting among families. On plantations, enslaved people traditionally received a brief respite from field work — usually three to five days. Mountain communities gathered for hog killing cooperatives and Christmas singing.
december.economic: Year-end. The crop-lien cycle closed and reopened. Families who cleared their debt had a moment of solvency; families who did not entered January in arrears.
december.weather_conditions: Cold — 36-44F in the valley, 30-38F on Sand Mountain. Hard freezes. Occasional snow, more frequent on Sand Mountain.
december.narrative_hook: Hog killing in December was when {ancestor_name} secured the family's winter protein — the hog scalded and scraped, the hams salted, the sausage ground, and the lard rendered, because every ounce mattered when January came.

calendar_eras.description: After the Civil War, the cotton calendar continued on the bottomland but under a radically different labor system. Enslaved people were free, but sharecropping and tenant farming replaced slavery with a system of debt that bound families to the land almost as effectively. The crop-lien system — credit from the merchant at ruinous interest against the fall crop — governed the rhythm of the year. The boll weevil reached the Tennessee Valley around 1916 and devastated cotton yields. Sand Mountain and the mountain counties continued their subsistence pattern, but even they were being pulled into the cash economy through small-scale tobacco and cotton production. Court terms at the county seat remained the primary civic gathering. The seasonal calendar's most important change was the addition of public school terms — fall and winter, designed around the agricultural calendar so children could work during planting and harvest.

january.agricultural: Fields dormant. Hog killing if not completed in December. Fence and building repair. Tenant farmers negotiating new contracts with landlords — December-January was the annual renegotiation period. A tenant unhappy with last year's settlement might move to a different landlord.
january.social_civic: Circuit court sessions. School in session (public schools ran roughly November-March to accommodate the agricultural calendar). Church services.
january.economic: Crop-lien credit negotiations. New tenancy contracts. The annual tenant shuffle — families moving between landlords — happened in late December through January.
january.weather_conditions: Cold — 36-42F in the valley. Hard freezes. Roads muddy.
january.narrative_hook: January was when {ancestor_name} decided whether to stay or move — whether the landlord's share was fair, whether the soil would hold, whether the next farm over offered a better deal. The tenant's year started with arithmetic, not plowing.

february.agricultural: Late winter fieldwork beginning. Breaking ground on warm days. Mountain farmers tapping trees and splitting rails.
february.social_civic: School in session. Church services. Limited social activity.
february.weather_conditions: Late winter — 38-48F. Ice storms the primary hazard.
february.narrative_hook: February in {county} County was the lean month — no income since fall, the store account running, and the only calendar that mattered was the one that counted days until planting.

march.agricultural: Plowing began. Cotton beds prepared on the bottomland. Corn planted. Garden plots turned. Tobacco plant beds started in mountain counties.
march.social_civic: School still in session. Spring court session. Easter.
march.economic: Peak credit extension at the crossroads store. Every farm family drawing on next fall's crop.
march.weather_conditions: Warming — 50-60F. Spring rains. Dogwood and redbud.
march.narrative_hook: March was when {ancestor_name} committed to the year — seed on credit, rent owed on the crop, and the merchant's book open to a fresh page that would fill with charges before a single boll opened.

april.agricultural: Cotton planting. Corn up. Garden vegetables planted. Mountain tobacco transplanted. Children pulled from school for planting — school terms ended by April in most rural districts.
april.social_civic: School closing for the season. Easter celebrations. Circuit rider visits.
april.economic: Full credit committed. No turning back.
april.weather_conditions: Warm — 60-70F. Late frost risk for cotton.
april.narrative_hook: When school let out in April, it was not vacation — {ancestor_name}'s children went from the schoolhouse to the field, because the cotton needed every hand the family could put behind a hoe.

may.agricultural: Cotton chopping. Corn cultivation. Garden production beginning. Tobacco growing in mountain patches.
may.social_civic: Decoration Day (cemetery cleaning and grave decoration) — a major community event in both valley and mountain. Church homecomings.
may.narrative_hook: Decoration Day in May was when {ancestor_name} cleaned the family graves and decorated them with flowers — the one day the living acknowledged the dead and the whole community gathered at the cemetery.

june.agricultural: Cotton growing. Corn tasseling. Second cultivation. Boll weevil damage visible by June after 1916 — the weevil punctured bolls before they could open.
june.social_civic: Long days. Front-porch visiting in valley towns. Mountain communities more isolated.
june.economic: No income. After 1916, boll weevil anxiety — would the crop survive?
june.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 78-86F. Tennessee River low in dry summers.
june.narrative_hook: After the boll weevil came in {year}, June was when {ancestor_name} walked the rows and counted punctured squares — each one a dollar that would not reach the gin, a payment that would not reach the merchant.

july.agricultural: Lay-by time on the bottomland. Mountain cultivation continued. Small-scale tobacco harvesting began in mountain counties.
july.social_civic: Fourth of July celebrations. Camp meetings and revivals began. The revival was the social and spiritual peak of the year in both valley and mountain communities.
july.economic: No income. Odd jobs during lay-by.
july.narrative_hook: The camp meeting in July was where {ancestor_name} found rest — not from the heat, which was relentless, but from the cotton, which for one week did not demand anything but patience.

august.agricultural: Cotton bolls forming. Mountain tobacco being cut and hung in barns. Corn approaching maturity.
august.social_civic: Revival season at peak. Baptist protracted meetings. Singing conventions on Sand Mountain — Sacred Harp singing gatherings that drew singers from across the region.
august.economic: Harvest anticipation. After 1916, boll weevil damage assessment — how much of the crop was lost?
august.weather_conditions: Hot — 80-90F. Humidity oppressive in the valley.
august.narrative_hook: August singing convention on Sand Mountain was where {ancestor_name} heard the shape-note hymns that had come down from the eighteenth century — voices in four parts, no instruments, the sound carrying across the plateau like something older than the cotton that surrounded it.

september.agricultural: Cotton picking began. All hands in the field. Mountain corn harvest. Tobacco stripping in mountain counties.
september.social_civic: Picking consumed all time. Church attendance minimal during harvest.
september.economic: First income. Cotton to the gin. The year's debt reckoning approaching.
september.narrative_hook: September was when {ancestor_name} learned what the boll weevil, the rain, and the sun had decided — the cotton picked, the sacks weighed, and the gin receipt telling a story the merchant would finish.

october.agricultural: Peak cotton picking. Corn cribbed. Hog fattening. Mountain tobacco to market. Sorghum pressing — sorghum mills on Sand Mountain processed cane for molasses.
october.social_civic: Fall court session. County fairs. Sorghum stir-offs on Sand Mountain — communal events around the sorghum mill.
october.economic: Primary income month. Cotton settlement. Debt reckoning.
october.narrative_hook: The sorghum stir-off on Sand Mountain was {ancestor_name}'s harvest festival — the mule walking the mill circle, the sweet smell of cooking cane, and jars of dark molasses that would sweeten a winter of cornbread and fatback.

november.agricultural: Late picking. Fields cleaned. Winter preparation.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving. Hunting season. Schools reopened (fall term beginning November in some districts).
november.economic: Final sales. Debt settlement. Contract renegotiation for the next year.
november.narrative_hook: November was when {ancestor_name} settled with the landlord and the merchant — and the answer determined whether the family entered the new year free or in debt.

december.agricultural: Hog killing. Meat curing. Firewood cutting. Fields dormant.
december.social_civic: Christmas. Church services. Family visiting. Sacred Harp Christmas singings on Sand Mountain.
december.economic: Year-end. Tenant contracts expired. The annual moving decision made.
december.weather_conditions: Cold — 36-44F in valley, 30-38F on Sand Mountain. Hard freezes. Snow on the mountain.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas on Sand Mountain was singing and hog meat — {ancestor_name} in a cold cabin with the family, the ham in the smokehouse, and the Sacred Harp book open to a hymn that had crossed the Atlantic with people who had nothing else to carry.

calendar_eras.description: The TVA and defense era broke the agricultural calendar's dominance for the first time. TVA dam construction (1933-1945) created year-round industrial employment. The reservoirs themselves created a new seasonal pattern — lake recreation, fishing seasons, and flood-control cycles replaced the river's old rhythm of flood and drought. Redstone Arsenal (1941) and the missile/space program (1950s) imposed an industrial-military calendar on Madison County that had nothing to do with cotton. But the transformation was uneven: Sand Mountain communities continued farming through the 1950s, and tenant farming persisted in parts of the valley until the 1940s. The new calendar was layered on top of the old one, and which calendar you lived by depended on where you were and who employed you.

january.agricultural_rural: Cotton farming still practiced in valley but declining. Some families still following the old calendar. Sand Mountain subsistence farming unchanged.
january.industrial_urban: Redstone Arsenal and defense plants operating year-round. TVA-managed reservoirs at winter pool levels. Industrial workers on twelve-month schedules — no seasonal layoff.
january.social_civic: School in session. Urban Huntsville developing civic calendar disconnected from agriculture.
january.economic: Dual economy: rural areas still on the crop-lien cycle; urban/defense areas on weekly wages. The gap between the two worlds widening.
january.weather_conditions: Cold — 36-42F. TVA reservoirs at lowest level (winter drawdown for flood control).
january.narrative_hook: By {year}, January in the Tennessee Valley meant two different things — for {ancestor_name} on Sand Mountain it was still the cold, lean month between harvests. For the engineer at Redstone, it was just another workweek.

february.agricultural_rural: Late winter fieldwork beginning for remaining farmers. TVA agricultural extension agents working with valley farmers on soil conservation.
february.industrial_urban: Defense work continuing. Engineering and testing schedules at Redstone Arsenal unaffected by weather.
february.economic: Rural credit cycle beginning. Urban wages steady.
february.weather_conditions: Late winter — 38-48F. TVA reservoirs beginning to fill with spring rains.
february.narrative_hook: TVA changed February in the Tennessee Valley — the river that used to flood without warning was now held behind dams, and the agricultural agent who showed up at {ancestor_name}'s farm had a government paycheck and a soil conservation plan.

march.agricultural_rural: Plowing and planting in remaining agricultural areas. TVA-promoted crop diversification — soybeans, hay, truck farming replacing cotton monoculture.
march.industrial_urban: Defense work at full capacity. Spring building season for contractors.
march.social_civic: Easter. Schools preparing for spring break. TVA-sponsored community programs.
march.economic: Rural credit cycle active. Urban economy expanding.
march.weather_conditions: Warming — 50-60F. TVA reservoirs rising toward summer pool.
march.narrative_hook: The TVA agent told {ancestor_name} to plant soybeans instead of cotton — the soil was exhausted, the boll weevil was relentless, and the dam had changed the drainage. The old calendar was dying, and the new one did not answer to any crop.

april.agricultural_rural: Planting season. Gardens. Some remaining cotton; increasing soybeans and diversified crops.
april.industrial_urban: Redstone Arsenal testing season. Rocket motor tests on the arsenal grounds audible across Madison County.
april.social_civic: Easter. Baseball season opening. Schools in session.
april.economic: Mixed economy: agricultural credit and industrial wages operating side by side in the same counties.
april.weather_conditions: Warm — 60-70F. TVA reservoirs approaching summer pool.
april.narrative_hook: In April {year}, {ancestor_name} could hear rocket motor tests from the arsenal echoing across Madison County bottomland that had grown cotton for 140 years — the future arriving with a roar that drowned out the plowing.

may.agricultural_rural: Cultivation. Garden production. Remaining cotton farmers following the old cycle.
may.industrial_urban: Defense work at full pace. Construction of new housing developments in Huntsville.
may.social_civic: Decoration Day. Memorial Day observances. Fishing season opening on TVA lakes.
may.economic: Lake recreation beginning to develop as economic activity — bait shops, boat docks, fishing camps along Wheeler Lake and Guntersville Lake.
may.weather_conditions: Hot — 70-80F. TVA lakes at summer pool — full and navigable.
may.narrative_hook: The TVA lakes changed May in {county} County — where {ancestor_name}'s grandfather had forded the Tennessee River with a cotton wagon, now there was a lake with a fishing dock and a bait shop.

june.agricultural_rural: Growing season. Reduced cultivation in areas shifting from cotton.
june.industrial_urban: Full defense production. Summer testing schedule at Redstone.
june.social_civic: Vacation season beginning — TVA lake recreation. Swimming, fishing, boating on the reservoirs.
june.economic: Lake recreation economy developing. Tourist cabins and fishing camps along Wheeler and Guntersville lakes.
june.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 78-86F. TVA lakes warm enough for swimming.
june.narrative_hook: June on Wheeler Lake was a world {ancestor_name}'s grandparents would not have recognized — a lake where the river used to be, recreational boats where cotton flatboats had gone, and families swimming in water that had once powered nothing but the current toward Muscle Shoals.

july.agricultural_rural: Lay-by for remaining cotton farmers. Mountain subsistence farming continuing. Tobacco harvest beginning in mountain patches.
july.industrial_urban: Arsenal work at pace. Fourth of July celebrations. Lake recreation at peak.
july.social_civic: Fourth of July fireworks over TVA lakes — a new community tradition. Camp meetings continuing in rural areas. Revival season in rural churches.
july.economic: Lake recreation economy at peak. Rural areas still on the old calendar.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 80-90F. TVA lakes at full summer pool.
july.narrative_hook: Fourth of July fireworks over Guntersville Lake in {year} lit up a world that TVA had invented — the dam, the lake, the electricity, and the fishing dock where corn rows had been.

august.agricultural_rural: Cotton approaching harvest in remaining areas. Mountain tobacco curing. Revival season.
august.industrial_urban: Defense work continuing. New school year approaching for Huntsville's growing population of engineers' children.
august.social_civic: Revival and singing conventions in rural areas. Sacred Harp singings on Sand Mountain. Urban Huntsville developing its own cultural calendar — lecture series, civic clubs.
august.economic: Dual economy continuing — harvest preparation in rural areas, steady wages in urban.
august.narrative_hook: In August {year}, the Tennessee Valley held two worlds at once — on Sand Mountain, {ancestor_name} sang shape-note hymns at the all-day singing. In Huntsville, a German rocket scientist adjusted calculations that would put Americans in space.

september.agricultural_rural: Cotton picking for remaining farmers. Corn harvest. School opening in rural districts.
september.industrial_urban: Full production. Arsenal employment at high levels.
september.social_civic: Schools opening across the region. County fairs.
september.economic: Rural harvest income. Urban wages steady.
september.weather_conditions: Moderating — 72-82F. TVA lakes beginning fall drawdown.
september.narrative_hook: September in the Tennessee Valley was when the two calendars overlapped — {ancestor_name} might pick cotton Monday through Friday and drive to Huntsville Saturday to see what the future looked like.

october.agricultural_rural: Peak harvest for remaining farmers. Sorghum pressing on Sand Mountain. Hunting season.
october.industrial_urban: Defense work continuing. Fall testing at Redstone Arsenal — rocket tests visible as contrails above the valley.
october.social_civic: County fairs. Homecoming at churches and schools. TVA lake fishing for fall species.
october.economic: Harvest income in rural areas. Defense payroll in urban Huntsville.
october.weather_conditions: Cool and pleasant — 58-68F. First frost late October. Best month in the valley.
october.narrative_hook: October in the Tennessee Valley was still the reckoning month for farmers — but by {year}, half the county was working for wages that did not depend on what the weather or the weevil decided.

november.agricultural_rural: Late harvest. Winter preparation. Hunting season at peak.
november.industrial_urban: Year-round production. Thanksgiving. Defense employment holiday schedules — paid holidays, a concept unknown to tenant farmers.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving. Hunting. Rural schools back in session.
november.economic: Agricultural income declining toward winter. Defense wages steady.
november.weather_conditions: Cold arriving — 44-54F. TVA reservoirs drawn down for winter flood control.
november.narrative_hook: Thanksgiving in {year} at {ancestor_name}'s table told the story of the Tennessee Valley's transformation — turkey from the Sand Mountain woods served alongside a paycheck from Redstone Arsenal.

december.agricultural_rural: Hog killing. Meat curing. Fields dormant. Sacred Harp Christmas singings on Sand Mountain.
december.industrial_urban: Arsenal operations reduced for holidays. Paid holiday leave — a revolutionary concept for families whose parents had never known a paid day off.
december.social_civic: Christmas. Church services. Family gatherings. New community traditions developing in Huntsville's growing suburbs.
december.economic: Rural year-end. Defense workers received paid holidays. The contrast between the two economies was sharpest in December — one calendar ending in debt settlement, the other in a paid vacation.
december.weather_conditions: Cold — 36-44F in the valley, 30-38F on Sand Mountain. Hard freezes.
december.narrative_hook: December in the Tennessee Valley was when {ancestor_name} saw the change most clearly — grandparents who settled hog killing and crop debts in the same week, and children who got paid holidays from a government job and bought Christmas presents with money that did not come from cotton.

narrative_hooks: The Tennessee Valley ran on two calendars — cotton on the bottomland and corn on the mountain — and which one governed {ancestor_name}'s year depended on whether the family farmed the rich river bottom or the thin Sand Mountain soil.

The boll weevil arrived around 1916 and broke the cotton calendar that had governed the valley for a century — {ancestor_name} watched the squares punctured and the income vanish, and the old rhythm never fully recovered.

TVA rewrote the calendar — the river that had flooded without warning was now held behind dams, the lakes covered what had been cornfields, and the seasons answered to reservoir management instead of the crop.
Camp meeting in July was the one week the cotton calendar released its grip — {ancestor_name} at the brush arbor hearing preaching that acknowledged the soul the crop ignored.

The sorghum stir-off on Sand Mountain was {ancestor_name}'s harvest festival — the mule circling the mill, the sweet smell of cooking cane, and jars of molasses that would carry the family through winter.
Hog killing in December was the pivot of the year — every family in the Tennessee Valley, bottomland planter and mountain yeoman alike, depended on the smokehouse to get through January.
source_references: Dupre, Daniel S. Transforming the Cotton Frontier: Madison County, Alabama, 1800-1840. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 1997.
Flynt, Wayne. Poor but Proud: Alabama's Poor Whites. Tuscaloosa: University of Alabama Press, 1989.
Flynt, Wayne. Alabama in the Twentieth Century. Tuscaloosa: University of Alabama Press, 2004.
McDonald, William Lindsey. A Walk Through the Past: People and Places of Florence and Lauderdale County, Alabama. Florence, AL: Bluewater Publications, 2003.
Owen, Thomas McAdory. History of Alabama and Dictionary of Alabama Biography. 4 vols. Chicago: S. J. Clarke, 1921.
